summary: Gruber examines the competing rumors about the next iPhone's design ahead of Apple's 'Let's Talk iPhone' event: either an iPhone 4-lookalike (dubbed 'iPhone 4S') or a MacBook Air-inspired teardrop design (dubbed 'iPhone 5'). He pushes back against the assumption that an iPhone 4S would be a letdown, noting the iPhone 4 is Apple's most popular device ever. His core design argument against the teardrop is that it would break the symmetry Apple has maintained across all iOS devices — a teardrop iPhone held in landscape would be unbalanced in weight and thickness. He examines the evidence on both sides, finding strong component evidence for an iPhone 4-style device but only case-maker speculation for the teardrop. He concludes with a Magic 8-Ball bit, expressing skepticism about the teardrop and leaving open the possibility of a completely secret new form factor.
key_points: The sentiment that an iPhone 4S would be a 'letdown' compared to an iPhone 5 is misguided — what matters is improved internals, not external novelty
There is strong component-leak evidence for an iPhone 4-style device but zero component evidence for a teardrop design
Symmetry is a fundamental design principle of Apple's iOS devices — they look and feel right in any orientation, which a teardrop shape would violate
A 4-inch display would drop below Apple's own retina display PPI threshold, making a larger screen unlikely
Apple may be planning a two-tier iPhone lineup: A5-powered high-end and A4-powered 8GB low-end, both in iPhone 4 form factor
money_quotes: I detect an undercurrent of sentiment that if Apple announces the iPhone 4S, it'd be a letdown, but if they announce the iPhone 5, it'd be exciting. But this is all merely about how the thing looks on the outside.
Why should an iPhone 4-lookalike 'iPhone 4S' be considered disappointing if it contains significantly improved components?
The iPhone 4 is, I think it fair to say, the most popular device Apple has ever made.
Symmetry is a hallmark of Apple's iOS devices to date. They look right in any orientation.
The MacBook Air is symmetric despite its teardrop shape because it has only one orientation.
A teardrop iPhone, when held in landscape, would be thicker (and presumably heavier) on one side than the other. That seems wrong to me.
That's a remarkable amount of effort to put into something that is, ultimately, useless. But, still — kind of cool, right?
Testing new internal components in disguised exteriors is an obvious strategy for a secretive company.
key_insight: The teardrop iPhone rumor fails Apple's own design test: iOS devices maintain symmetry so they look and feel balanced in any orientation, a principle a tapered form factor would violate.
